Alex Hormozi pointed out that this reflected the favorable Austin supply-demand dynamic for med spas rather than a scalable acquisition capability, and that the owner should build paid acquisition skills to avoid competitive vulnerability when market conditions normalize.

The nonprofit health insurance broker in this session used to work at Berkshire Hathaway (Warren Buffett's firm) and directly heard Buffett and Charlie Munger describe the U.S. health insurance industry as the most broken model in America. The speaker built a nonprofit business model to exploit this: rather than taking a 5-10% commission on premium (the industry standard), the firm takes approximately 20% of the savings it generates for clients, meaning the firm only earns money when the client pays less. At the time of the session, the business was two years old and generating $3M revenue through a network of 170 affiliate referral partners (primarily CPAs and financial advisors), yet only closing roughly one new client account per month, indicating the affiliate channel was almost entirely inactive.

The broker described a prospecting scenario where he pitches a business with 100 employees and immediately claims he can save them $200,000 (roughly $2,000 per employee on the low side). This number is real and deliverable, but prospects immediately react with skepticism and disengage, interpreting the claim as too good to be true. Alex Hormozi confirmed this reaction is predictable and not a product of the prospect's sophistication but of the pitch framing: the claim provides a big number with no credible mechanism, triggering a scam heuristic.

The broker noted that a former employer, Procter & Gamble (P&G), had a 0% effective tax rate on certain activities but never disclosed this publicly because all details were under NDA. This illustrates the structural proof constraint: even when results are dramatic and verifiable, the clients most likely to provide compelling testimonials (large enterprises with significant savings) are precisely those who have the most to lose from public disclosure, because publicizing tax minimization strategies invites regulatory and public-relations scrutiny.

Alex Hormozi illustrates the behavioral definition of learning with a phone-answering example: a trainer teaches a trainee to answer the phone with script DEF instead of their habitual ABC. The phone rings (same condition). If the trainee says DEF, learning has occurred — observable behavior changed under the same condition. If the trainee reverts to ABC, no learning has occurred. If one trainee says DEF on the first ring and another requires five rings before saying DEF, the first trainee is more intelligent by this definition.

Alex Hormozi uses podcast consumption as the primary illustration: any person who has listened to a thousand podcasts but remains in the exact same life condition with the exact same behaviors has not learned. They have entertained themselves while believing they were learning. By the behavioral definition, they have also demonstrated low intelligence — not because of innate capacity, but because they failed to change behavior under repeated exposure to the same triggering conditions.

Alex Hormozi illustrates reference-point-driven motivation with a wealth comparison: a person worth $100M whose social circle consists entirely of billionaires perceives themselves as relatively deprived — $900M behind their reference group. A person earning $5,000/month who wants $10,000/month perceives only a $5,000/month gap. By Hormozi's deprivation model, the $100M person is more motivated to earn money than the $5,000/month earner, because their perceived deprivation is larger, even though their absolute wealth is vastly greater. This explains why many objectively poor people are not strongly motivated while some extremely wealthy people remain intensely hungry — the reference point, not the absolute number, determines drive.

At an event, a young woman asked Alex Hormozi to give her 60 seconds of motivation. He responded by asking her to define motivation first. She could not. Hormozi used this to illustrate that asking for something you cannot define means you have no way to evaluate whether you received it — making the request incoherent. He describes this as the core problem with undefined abstract terms in conversation.
topic: Undefined Words Create Mythology and Prevent Clear Thinking

text: Alex Hormozi recounts that by always aiming to be the best student and best success story of every program he purchased, he consistently over-delivered — which then caused each program vendor to publicly claim credit for his outcomes. He describes this as having backfired because roughly ten different people have at various times publicly claimed they were the catalyst behind his launch success, even though the results were driven by his own execution.

Alex Hormozi and Leila Hormozi were accepted to a super-premium network that required only one member per partnership. The network operators first said no to both joining, then privately asked Leila (or Alex) to lie about the arrangement so both could get in while circumventing the rules. The Hormozis declined: they refused to lie and required a package deal or nothing, ultimately walking away from the network entirely to preserve their integrity standard.

Alex Hormozi’s first high-ticket sale came in 2015-2016 during a period when he was closing gym launches at $500 for a 16-week program. Facing eight gym calls he intended to cancel, he set a price he expected the first gym owner to reject: $6,000 (a 12x increase from his usual $500). The gym owner said yes immediately. On the next call he charged $8,000, then $10,000 per subsequent call, and by the end of one day had collected $60,000. This experience broke his price-belief ceiling. He recounts this as the moment his earning trajectory fundamentally changed.

Alex Hormozi cited a health-space company he worked with where he believed the offer was mispriced well below market. He pushed for a doubling of price, which the company resisted; he was able to implement a 50% price increase instead. Contrary to expectations of reduced sales, close rates increased. His explanation: the original price was so low relative to what the offer promised (medical-grade outcomes, doctor-reviewed protocols) that prospects did not believe it could work.
topic: Close Rate as Pricing Calibration Signal

text: Alex Hormozi contrasts Tesla’s market entry strategy with Honda’s brand position to illustrate top-down versus bottom-up brand building. Tesla began with the $250,000 Roadster in limited production, then introduced the Model S, and eventually the Model 3 and Model Y at accessible price points. This sequence allowed each new product to be positioned as an affordable version of a premium brand. Hormozi argues Honda attempting to launch a luxury vehicle would fail because their brand narrative is anchored at the affordable end, making upward repositioning nearly impossible.

Alex Hormozi recounts learning about management consulting, private equity, and investment banking for the first time at Vanderbilt University. Growing up in Baltimore, his reference for a wealthy person was a doctor — a category his own father occupied. Arriving at Vanderbilt, he felt poor relative to classmates from New York and California, realizing his mental model of wealth had been entirely shaped by the limited circle around him. He uses this autobiographically to illustrate why people sell from their own wallet: they have only ever been exposed to low-wealth reference points.

A founder running marketing services to both restaurants and chauffeur transportation companies ($1M/year revenue) discovered that chauffeur clients were costing $30-$50 to acquire versus $600 for restaurant clients. Alex Hormozi advised the founder not to make any structural changes yet, because the two-week-old chauffeur campaign lacked LTV data. Hormozi instructed the founder to wait approximately one month to gather LTV-to-CAC proof before deciding whether to sunset the restaurant business or the chauffeur business. The core decision criteria: if the chauffeur segment's LTV-to-CAC matches the restaurant segment, sunset the restaurant business and focus entirely on chauffeurs.

Alex Hormozi described running ads for personal trainers using his gym business's LTV data, expecting to print money because personal trainer acquisition costs appeared very low. The reality: personal trainers had no money, were a pain to serve, and produced poor LTV. Hormozi uses this as a canonical example of why cheap CAC is not sufficient evidence of a good customer segment; you must reach the LTV stage of data to know whether the segment is genuinely profitable.

Alex Hormozi recounts an encounter at a social gathering where a man accompanying a mutual friend raised concerns about overtraining. Hormozi replied sharply that overtraining is only a legitimate concern once you actually look like you work out. He acknowledges the comment was unkind but reports that the man subsequently changed his training approach and gained muscle — illustrating that challenging a false capacity ceiling produced a real adaptation response.

Alex Hormozi describes losing everything financially, then rebuilding and earning more in the following 12 months than before the loss. This experience produced the frame shift that no work is wasted because the individual is the output — not the money earned or the business outcomes. The rebuild demonstrated that work capacity, once built, survives the destruction of external results.
topic: Work as Identity and Capability Formation

text: A real estate services founder running ~$17-18M in annual revenue described spending 20 hours per week personally closing sales meetings, along with his COO, together generating roughly $5M per year in external revenue. Despite repeatedly trying to remove himself from the closing process, reps continued to request his involvement for every appointment. Alex Hormozi diagnosed this as a founder-driven sales process problem: the founder's script relied on authority and consultative expertise rather than a structured questioning sequence the prospect could be led through independently.

Alex Hormozi illustrated the distinction between a founder close and a scalable rep close using Acquisition.com as an example. When a prospect on a sales call asks what specifically Alex or Acquisition.com will help them with, a rep cannot answer the way Alex would — by discussing a bespoke paid-ads or webinar strategy with specific hooks and angles. Instead the rep's only job is to confirm the prospect has a real pain ('you have a heart that needs surgery') and that the business solves it, then ask if the prospect wants to proceed. The specific mechanic of the solution is irrelevant to the close.

Alex Hormozi uses Tesla as the canonical illustration of the top-down business launch sequence. Tesla launched with the Roadster — a $250,000 car with very limited production and high profitability per unit. Tesla then moved to the Model S, a still-premium but higher-volume vehicle, and subsequently to the mass-market Model 3 and Model Y. This sequence allowed Tesla to anchor its brand at the highest quality tier, build operational capacity progressively, and frame every successive car as 'an affordable version of our premium product' rather than as a budget vehicle. Hormozi notes that the reverse — starting as a budget manufacturer and then trying to sell a $250,000 car — would not carry the same brand credibility.

Alex Hormozi uses Netflix, Spotify, and Tesla as examples of mass-market low-price consumer businesses that nearly went bankrupt multiple times and required externally inflated capital to survive. Netflix and Spotify charge roughly $13 per month and must produce world-class entertainment across many genres just to justify that subscription; Tesla is a capital-intensive manufacturer. All three required venture and institutional capital to prop them up until they could achieve the volume needed to break even — illustrating that bootstrapped operators cannot imitate this model.

Alex Hormozi gives the example of a business owner who currently charges $1,000 for their core product and believes the natural next price point is $1,500. Hormozi argues this is wrong: the buyer does not think in 50% increments. The correct next tier is $5,000 or $10,000, which is the next rung on the pricing ladder where the buyer perceives a categorically different offer.

Alex Hormozi illustrates the identity-as-behavior model by noting that describing who someone is immediately reduces to describing what they do: if asked who John is, the first response is to describe what John does. He uses the example of a carpenter and a podcaster to show that these are simply behavioral labels, not metaphysical essences, and that the woo-woo notion of being as separate from doing confuses people unnecessarily.

Alex Hormozi describes his own experience being stuck in the ROAS doom loop for three and a half years. During that period, he kept scaling ad spend, revenues rose, but margins compressed steadily as spend moved to less-profitable audience tiers. The loop continued until spend roughly equaled revenue with no meaningful net return. He uses this personal history to argue the DTC founder can either spend three and a half more years learning this lesson or accept the diagnosis immediately.

Alex Hormozi uses a visual metaphor of a ladder with rungs to illustrate how paid traffic efficiency degrades with scale: at the first rung, a small amount of spend generates a given number of sales; at each higher rung, the same number of sales requires proportionally more spend because the brand is buying progressively less-efficient audience tiers. The gap between spend and revenue narrows at each rung until they converge.

Alex Hormozi invokes a CMO of Chubbies who wrote an article describing the direct-response doom loop: brands keep spending more to make less, then respond by spending more again, in a self-reinforcing cycle that ends with no net profit despite high revenue. He cites this as external validation that the pattern the DTC founder is experiencing is a well-documented structural trap, not a tactical execution failure.

Alex Hormozi uses the Gymshark and Sam Sulek ambassador relationship as a concrete illustration of brand spend that produces no measurable 30-day ROAS but is strategically rational. Gymshark pays Sam Sulek money knowing he cannot directly drive trackable conversions; the investment works because repeated co-presentation with a high-status fitness figure transfers positive associations to the Gymshark brand across the target audience over time.

Alex Hormozi uses a bartender in a busy bar as the canonical illustration of context-dependent status. In the bar, the bartender controls the scarce resource (alcohol and service access) and receives tips and attention on every small gesture. Outside the bar, walking down the street, the same person controls no scarce resource and receives no deference — not tips, not waving money, not attention. The example illustrates that status is not a personal property but an environmental one that exists only while the person controls the reinforcer.

Alex Hormozi contrasts a trust-fund kid (status only: inherited money = controls a resource others want) with a trust-fund kid who also gave ten specific cryptocurrency picks that all subsequently increased in value. The second person has both status (money) and power (say-do correspondence with positive outcome). Hormozi argues the second person would generate dramatically more influence over the first because they have demonstrated predictive accuracy that conditions future compliance, illustrating why Power is the single component he would choose if forced to select only one.

Alex Hormozi cites the launch of his book ($100M+ Leads) as a single event that checked multiple SPCL boxes simultaneously: the documented $100M+ in sales generated status (money/asset control), the observable scale of the launch generated credibility (hard to fake, visible with your eyes), and providing actionable launch advice that people then successfully followed generated power. He uses this to illustrate that high-leverage singular real-world events are more efficient influence-builders than piece-by-piece content strategies.

A restaurant business owner at an Acquisition.com event disclosed operating five different restaurant concepts across six locations doing $25M+ in revenue and targeting $100M. He acknowledged that what was blocking growth was himself and his business partner rather than external factors, and asked Hormozi how to justify marketing spend to his finance partner by finding a CAC number. Hormozi used this interaction to illustrate that seeking a traditional CAC metric was misaligned with how restaurants grow: the real CAC is the premium paid in service, ingredients, and systems that drives organic word-of-mouth, not an advertising budget. The owner's instinct to solve the growth problem through marketing was reframed as a product-quality and experience problem.

Hormozi explained that while most restaurant customers do not respond to Instagram buy-one-get-one promotions in steady-state, a grand opening is the exception where a restaurant operator should spend heavily on multi-channel ads, overstaffing, and promotional giveaways. For premium concepts, the giveaways shift to exclusive invite-only formats, but the underlying promotional activity is the same. The purpose of this initial push is to seed enough initial traffic so that word-of-mouth from satisfied customers carries the location to full capacity within six months without further advertising.

A real estate wholesaling business with 17 reps, Google Ads inbound leads, and a compensation structure of $42K base salary plus 10% of assignment fees was averaging $450K/month in gross revenue, had achieved $700K months in the same year, but repeatedly crashed to $200-250K in the month immediately following a peak month. The owner attributed the crashes to reps taking it easy after earning $20K-$30K in a single big month. Alex Hormozi diagnosed the root cause as a sales ops and culture problem: scripts were being treated as guidelines rather than requirements, there was no daily training cadence, and the sales manager was not enforcing process consistency. The business had consistent inbound lead quality month to month, ruling out lead variation as the cause.

Alex Hormozi described the group screening funnel for high-volume sales hires: applicants receive the script before the interview and are asked to submit a 60-second video reciting it. Those who submit are invited to a group call (not individual calls, to save management time), where they are drilled on the script and given feedback. If a candidate takes the feedback and improves their delivery, they advance to a job offer call. If a candidate shows up unprepared or reacts with attitude to coaching feedback, they are immediately eliminated from consideration. From a starting group of ten candidates, two or three typically advance to job offers. Hormozi noted that the rigor of the process itself communicates the company's culture and filters for coachability and work ethic.
Restaurant menu offering still or sparkling water presents binary choice as autonomy when both options are pre-selected by the restaurant; customer perceives choice but has not selected the frame.

Alex Hormozi describes a personal experience early in his career where he consistently felt he was not good enough and did not deserve success. He recounts the moment he realized that despite this feeling of inadequacy, he could still take the required actions and still get results. He describes the experience as feeling like he was getting away with something: he could take action, feel unworthy, and the outcome still materialized. This realization was, by his account, a major breakthrough that freed him psychologically to continue acting despite persistent self-doubt.

Hormozi references a viral clip from approximately three years before the recording (from a Video Growth Summit) in which he publicly described the three co-occurring traits of hyper-successful people: grandiose inflated superiority, crippling insecurity, and impulse control. He notes that early in his own career he identified more with the insecurity side than the grandiosity side, saying he started with 'this could happen for me' rather than a full superiority complex.

Alex Hormozi illustrates three levels of leverage using cold calling as the base input. Level 1: a founder does 200 cold calls per day and generates a certain number of sales personally. Level 2: the founder hires someone to make those 200 calls for them, generating the same sales without founder effort, but output is flat. Level 3: the founder spends one week making 200 calls per day, but the goal of those calls is to recruit a recruiter. That recruiter then brings one new caller per week. Each week, another 200 calls per day are added to the stack permanently, causing cumulative sales to grow indefinitely from a one-week personal effort. The third level produces compounding input without ongoing founder labor.

Alex Hormozi uses a fitness analogy to illustrate the order-of-magnitude gap in business: a person working out five minutes a week and wondering why they are not losing weight is doing the right activity but at a volume so far below the necessary threshold that even doubling to ten minutes per week still produces no result. The solution is not incremental adjustment but a 10x or 100x increase. In fitness, people intuitively understand roughly how much exercise is required; in business, this context is absent, making the volume shortfall invisible.

Alex Hormozi uses the example of a father who did not hug his child enough, creating shame in the child. If that shame became the catalyst for the child to start exercising regularly, living 20 years longer as a result, it illustrates that the negative origin of a motivational driver is irrelevant to whether it produces a positive life outcome. The quality of the source is judged by what it generates, not by how it feels.

Hormozi describes a common life-decline trajectory to illustrate cascading setback failure: a man loses his job (one adverse event), then gains weight, and observers note that 'he's just never been the same.' The person did not decline because the job loss was catastrophic in isolation; he declined because the initial event was not contained and instead spread into health, self-image, and relationships, each degradation compounding the next.

For the Money Models launch, Alex Hormozi's team produced 2,800 ads before the six-week launch period began. With only 5 editors producing 25 ads per day, they could not have built that library; they contracted 10 additional editors (15 total) to meet the required output rate. At peak spend during the final days of the launch, the team was spending $500,000 per day on ads. The launch generated over $105 million. Hormozi's framing: the cost of contracting the additional editors was trivially small relative to $105M, making the decision obvious once the math was done.

Alex Hormozi uses the example of a business doing one sale per week, with sales appearing on Monday one week, Thursday the next, Friday the week after, and Tuesday the following week — seemingly random. His interpretation: the randomness reflects a fixed but low advertising level. To reach one sale per day (7 sales per week), the business would need to 7x its advertising volume; accounting for likely efficiency loss at higher volume, it might require 14x. The point: volatile sales are a volume problem, not a market or offer problem.

Alex Hormozi uses the analogy of committing to 100 calls per day as a forcing function for optimization: once locked into that volume, an operator naturally starts noticing that prospects pick up more in the afternoon, or between 5-7am in certain markets. The pain of doing 100 inefficient calls motivates time-of-day and segment analysis — optimization that would not have occurred without the volume commitment first.

Alex Hormozi used himself preparing a presentation as an example of standards evolution. He recalled that his first presentation had 19 slides with four bullets each and that he considered the one full day (~8 hours) he spent on it to be substantial effort at the time. He contrasted this with his current standard, in which he counts work in hundreds of hours and does not consider something done until he can no longer improve it.

Alex Hormozi recounted his book production process as a concrete instance of the quality-bounded definition of done. He completed 19 drafts and 4 full rewrites, wrote the entire manuscript at a third-grade reading level, and inserted 106 hand-drawn images. He described reading it front to back repeatedly until he could find nothing more to remove, add, or clarify. He stated: if it does not succeed, that's fine, but it will not be for lack of effort.

Alex Hormozi posed a thought experiment to illustrate how time allocation determines quality ceiling in creative work. He described giving an editor a short video clip with 30 minutes to complete it; at each stage he asked if another 30 minutes would yield improvement, then 2 additional hours, then 2 weeks. At each stage the editor confirmed they could make it better. After 2 weeks, the editor said they would restructure the entire piece. Hormozi used this to show that most submitted work is far below the producer's own attainable ceiling when time constraints are lifted.

Alex Hormozi used the example of Uber and Lyft to illustrate the market-size gap between a first-place and second-place competitor. Despite Uber and Lyft competing in the same ride-sharing category and delivering essentially the same service, Uber is approximately 10x the size of Lyft. He used this to argue that the difference between being number one and number two in a market is not a small incremental gap but a factor-of-ten or greater structural advantage.

Alex Hormozi cited Olympic racing as a concrete instance of how a trivially small absolute performance gap (roughly one tenth of a second between gold and silver) produces a categorically different reward outcome. The gold medalist receives all endorsements, legacy, and category status; the silver medalist receives comparatively little. He used this to support his argument that competitive markets reward relative rank, not absolute performance.

Alex Hormozi spent five years building a gym chain to six locations, then sold five of them for a large (relative to prior earnings) payout. He reinvested the proceeds into a new venture. His partner in that venture took the money, disappeared, and filed bankruptcy, effectively transferring the funds to his girlfriend in Sweden — leaving no legal recourse. Hormozi describes initially feeling that he had wasted five years. However, in the 12 months that followed, he generated more profit than in the entire prior five-year period multiplied by approximately five. He attributes this outcome solely to the accumulated skills, judgment, and operational experience he had built through each successive gym location — learnings that traveled with him even after the financial loss.

During a conversation described in the transcript, Hormozi's interlocutor observes that people misread Hormozi's work volume as sacrifice. Hormozi agrees and explains that the misunderstanding stems from the observer projecting their own experience of work as cost. For Hormozi, the activity done for work is the same activity he would choose for recreation — so the high volume does not register as deprivation.

Alex Hormozi describes a training partner who, during his hardest sets (money sets) in the gym, does not want encouragement like you got this but instead asks to be told you're fine. Hormozi explains this as the partner self-applying factual confirmation rather than motivational language to get through peak difficulty, because you're fine is evidence-based (you are not dying) whereas you got this is outcome-based (you will succeed).

Alex Hormozi recounts that before pledging a fraternity in the SEC (Southeastern Conference, known for heavy hazing), he called his father to voice anxiety about what might happen. His father responded: There is nothing they can do to you that you have not already had worse happen. Hormozi used this as a mental frame going into the pledge process and found each hazing challenge comically manageable by comparison to what he had already survived. He describes this as a real-world demonstration of the worst-case baseline principle: because his personal floor was already set high, new threats measured against it lost their power to generate fear.

Alex Hormozi illustrates the definition of learning with a door-answering exercise: a trainer tells someone to answer the door with 'welcome to Starbucks' instead of their habitual greeting. On the next knock, if the person says 'welcome to Starbucks,' learning has occurred (same condition: knock-knock; new behavior: Starbucks greeting). If they revert to the old greeting, no learning occurred. The number of knock-knock trials required before the person consistently says 'welcome to Starbucks' measures how intelligent they are relative to the task.

Alex Hormozi describes his time at Vanderbilt University (ranked approximately top 5-7 in the US at the time of the video), where he perceived himself as having below-average intelligence relative to his classmates. His solution was to work from 9 AM to 9 PM every day exclusively in the library, pausing only for class, the cafeteria, and the gym. By running more iterations per day than his peers, he was able to advance faster than people he believed had superior intellectual capacity: his 10 compressed iterations outpaced their 3 slower iterations on a calendar basis.
topic: Effort Compounds Relative to Starting Conditions

text: Alex Hormozi describes the Gym Launch consumable media model: Gym Launch spent roughly $50,000 renting models, shooting ad content, editing, and testing approximately 100 ads across 10 to 20 representative markets segmented by demographic and operator quality. The top 5 winners were then delivered monthly to gym members, who could either run those proven ads immediately or spend their own money trying to find winners independently. Hormozi frames this as handing members a winning scratch-off ticket to insert into a machine (the gym's ad infrastructure) they had already paid for once.

Alex Hormozi cites a wholesaling teacher who, instead of just teaching students how to find properties, had his team research, vet, and rate deals every month and compiled them into a PDF delivered to his community. All the student had to do was drive to the address. Hormozi notes that if a member closed even one deal per year from the list, it covered the entire annual membership fee — making the value proposition mathematically obvious.

Alex Hormozi describes a print-on-demand community with approximately 2% monthly churn. The consumable media was a weekly or monthly list of trending print-on-demand products and logos, researched and compiled by the community operator using their own tools. Members received a ready-to-use trend list rather than having to do their own product research. Hormozi cites the extremely low churn as evidence that this consumable media delivery model dramatically anchors member retention.

Hormozi describes the ink-printer metaphor to illustrate the system-plus-inputs model: you teach someone how to do hair extensions (sell the system once), and then they buy your hair extensions every month for their customers. Or you teach them how to sell supplements and then they purchase your supplements monthly for resale. The initial education sale is a one-time event; the recurring supply of inputs is the repeating revenue engine.

Hormozi contrasts the cookbook analogy against the consumable media model: most membership operators give members a cookbook (methodology) but leave them without flour, eggs, an oven, or pots and pans (the inputs). The gap between having the recipe and being able to execute it is where most members fail and churn. He argues the fortune is in closing that gap — figuring out the missing inputs and delivering them pre-packaged.

Alex Hormozi describes his gym accountability system as a three-tier product: peer-to-peer accountability (a fellow member at the same stage suffering the same challenges), alumni accountability (a member who has completed the journey and cheers from the finish line), and expert accountability (a coach who has helped thousands of similar clients and can diagnose and break through any plateau). He describes this as the sales pitch he used to sell the accountability system at his gyms, attributing 75% of perceived value to accountability versus fitness or nutrition content.

An airport bus service is universally perceived as an inconvenience—passengers expect an air bridge to the terminal and feel cheated when the bus arrives instead. A pilot at Gatwick airport reframed the bus by announcing: "The good news is the bus will take you all the way to passport control so you won't have far to walk with your bags." This is objectively always true, but the new frame inverted the meaning: the bus became a conveyance that saved passengers from walking past retail stands with luggage, rather than an inferior alternative to an air bridge. The same situation, told through a different narrative, shifted from negative to positive.
